Once a company code is productive or live with transactional data, any changes become restrictive. Moving configuration changes without transforming the already posted documents can lead to data inconsistencies in the live system, potentially causing critical disruptions in open business processes.

To separate any Company Code(s) from the live system, both the configuration, master and it’s associated transactional data must be handled consistently. For deletion of existing posted documents or transforming open documents, the SAP DMLT Company Code Carve Out Service is required. For more information, refer to the Company Code Carve Out Solution Brief.

Such data transformations in a live production system demand a highly precise and controlled changeover, ensuring consistency at the database level and seamless continuity of SAP software operations. Therefore, every relevant business scenario should be carefully reviewed in the system, considering current configurations and customizations across all connected application modules.

SAP DMLT offers a technical feasibility study to outline system data dependencies and prerequisite conditions. This is followed by a detailed customer workshop with the obtained statistics from the system. The realization of the transformation requirement is delivered under a separate professional consulting contract and executed as a project.

The overall delivery process is broadly divided into two parts:

1. Pre-Qualification of Requirement

It is essential to pre-qualify the business requirement to ensure the right-fit transformation solution is offered. This starts with gathering preliminary details about the business requirement and application usage within the system through a technical questionnaire, available at Mergers and Acquisitions, Divestments, Restructuring.

Based on the received responses, SAP can propose a feasibility study to assess the system, evaluate overall complexity, and deliver a detailed proposal. This assessment typically takes around two weeks, including the deployment of analysis tools in the system.

An SAP Solution Architect/Expert will perform the system assessment and present the key insights during a customer workshop session. This is followed by the provision of commercial details through the designated local SAP Market Unit account representatives.

2. Project Delivery

Depending on the complexity of the engagement, the required number of mock execution cycles will be determined during the scoping phase. In general, data transformation projects of this nature involve a minimum of 2-3 mock cycles before the production data cutover. The purpose of a mock execution is to simulate the complete production data carve-out/deletion, validate the results, and estimate the overall downtime required for the production system.

Each mock execution cycle includes several phases, which can be broadly classified as follows:

2.1 Technical Preparation Phase of Add-On (Uptime):

These types of transformation capabilities are deployed into the customer system as a separate add-on, delivered as a transportable content. The mock execution system should be a recent copy of the production system, with identical or closely matching hardware configuration that of the production environment. Once the tool content is deployed, the technical preparation phase begins, during which SAP DMLT experts prepare the transformation tool. This phase typically takes 2–3 weeks and includes uptime activities and the generation of technical programs for the relevant table-fields.

2.2 Data Carve-Out Phase (Downtime):

After the uptime preparatory activities are completed, the agreed business downtime begins. During this period, Company Code Carve-Out is performed directly at the database table-field level. All relevant Company Codes are separated along with the relevant Org Units in the respective application areas (e.g., FI-GL, FI-AA, ML, CO etc).

2.3 Post-Processing Testing Phase (Uptime):

Once the data carve-out phase is completed, the transformed system is handed over to the customer’s IT/application team for a sanity check during downtime. Upon successful completion of the sanity check, the transformed system is reopened for normal business operations.

Engagement with relevant SAP team or SAP Experts

If your business requirement involves any transformation related to Company Code aspects in a live, productive SAP ECC or S/4HANA system, you can engage with the SAP Data Management and Landscape Transformation (DMLT) consulting team through following ways;

  1. Create a Support Incident
    Submit an incident via the SAP ONE Support Launchpad under the component: CA-LT-SRV (Landscape Transformation Services), Or
  2. Contact via Email
    Reach out directly to the DMLT Global Consulting Experts Email: sap_dmlt_gce@sap.com

The SAP DMLT team will guide you through the pre-assessment process, feasibility evaluation, and project-based service engagement tailored to your specific system and transformation needs.

Reference SAP OSS Notes:

481938 - System Landscape Optimization Services

1462004 - SAP LT: Transformation Solution 'Company Carve-out'

Disclaimer: This blog is intended only for knowledge sharing about Company Code Carve-out/Delete service on SAP ERP (ECC or S/4 HANA) system which might help customer in their landscape transformation requirements. It should not be considered as guideline for performing such transformation services on their own in any of the SAP landscape. Any data transformation efforts should be thoroughly assessed in consultation with qualified subject matter experts, supported by a comprehensive system study, and aligned with the appropriate approvals from your auditors and relevant stakeholders.

entry.title: Business Data Validation in SAP’s Data Transformation Journey: Comparative Overview of LTVF and DTV
entry.content:
Data is at the center of every SAP transformation. Whether the journey involves a system conversion to SAP S/4HANA, a landscape consolidation, data carve-out or a selective data transition, one question always remains critical: 

How do we ensure that business data is complete, accurate, and consistent after transformation? 

Manual reconciliation across systems, company codes, plants, and financial reports is not scalable in modern projects. To address this, SAP provides structured validation approaches that automate data checks and reduce testing effort.  

SAP transformation scenarios like system conversions, landscape consolidations, or selective migrations generate large volumes of business data that must move from source to target system without any scope for loss of data or inconsistency. Manual reconciliation of hundreds of reports is not only time consuming but also error prone. Automated frameworks help teams verify that migrated data aligns with expectations before commitment to production, thus reducing project risk and effort. 

Two key tool-based validation approaches used in SAP transformation programs are: 

  • Landscape Transformation Validation Framework (LTVF) 
  • Data Transition Validation (DTV) 

This blog explains what each approach is, how it works, and where it brings value within SAP transformation initiatives. 

Landscape Transformation Validation Framework (LTVF) 

The Landscape Transformation Validation Framework (LTVF) is a structured validation framework designed to support complex transformation scenarios. It is particularly relevant when data is not simply moved, but restructured, merged, split, or reorganized across systems and organizational units. 

Purpose of LTVF 

LTVF helps reduce test effort and downtime during transformation projects by automating the extraction, comparison, and evaluation of business data before and after transformation activities. 

It is commonly used in scenarios such as: 

  • Selective data transition projects 
  • S/4HANA implementation or conversion programs 
  • Mergers and acquisitions 
  • System consolidations or carve-outs 
  • Chart of accounts harmonization 
  • Organizational restructuring (plants, company codes, controlling areas) and several other scenarios

In these cases, validation is not always a simple “before equals after” check. Data may be aggregated, remapped, or reorganized according to defined transformation rules. LTVF supports this level of complexity. 

How LTVF Works

LTVF operates as a configurable validation framework. The typical process includes: 

1. Defining validation objects 
These may include SAP reports (ALV outputs), table contents, multiple tables join contents, text-based reports, custom reports, custom tables extractions etc. 

2. Setting filters and parameters 
For example, validating with respect to dates, fiscal years or specific organizational units like company codes, or plants etc. 

3. Customer Tailored Scenarios 

  • Full table or selective fields are supposed to be compared. 
  • Cross-table comparison between source and target landscapes. For Example: KONV to be considered from source and PRCD_ELEMENTS to be compared in the target. 
  • Ensure Negative testing by enabling a result format to hold the out-of-scope records. 
  • As it provides field level comparison, even a space of difference can be identified. 
  • Also has the flexibility to accept expected differences, compare substrings, concatenations, tolerances and much more 

4. Applying business logic 
LTVF allows the definition of transformation aware rules, for example: 

  • Renumbering certain fields involved during migration 
  • Aggregation logic after company code mergers 
  • Mapping validations after chart of account changes 
  • Tolerance thresholds for acceptable variances, etc. 
  • In case of consolidation scenarios, a merged result may be required 
  • Any custom logics implemented 

5. Automated comparison and result evaluation 
The framework highlights deviations and allows structured analysis across validation cycles. Because it supports rule-based interpretation of transformed data, LTVF can validate outcomes that are not expected to be identical at field level but must still align from a business perspective. 

Key Benefits of LTVF 

  • Significant reduction in manual reconciliation effort 
  • Structured validation across multiple transformation cycles 
  • Reduced downtime during cutover 
  • Support for complex restructuring logic 
  • Provides a tailored validation approach with respect to each customer 
  • Applicability across master data, transactional data, and configuration data 
  • Works seamlessly with a huge volume of data. 
  • High level statistic report will be available with the tool. 
  • Supports multiple test cycles before go-live 

Data Transition Validation (DTV) 

The Data Transition Validation (DTV) tool is SAP’s standard solution for automated data comparison during system conversions, selective transitions, and system upgrades. DTV is widely used in projects moving to SAP S/4HANA, especially in system conversion, upgrades and lean selective data transition scenarios. 

Purpose of DTV 

DTV is designed to validate that business data remains consistent before and after a system transition. It provides a structured way to: 

  • Capture data snapshots from a source system 
  • Capture corresponding data snapshots from a target system 
  • Automatically compare and highlight differences 
  • Handles large volumes of data 

How DTV Works 

The DTV process typically follows these steps: 

  1. Creation of a validation project 
    A project is defined in the DTV tool, specifying scope and validation objects. 
  2. Selection of validation content 
    SAP delivers predefined validation report content covering key functional areas such as Finance, Materials Management, Sales etc. Customers can also configure custom ALV reports and transparent table comparisons. 
  3. Parameter definition 
    Business filters are applied — for example: Company code, Fiscal year, Date range 
  4. Snapshot extraction 
    Data is extracted from the source system before transition and from the target system after transition. 
  5. Automated comparison and analysis 
    The tool identifies mismatches and provides structured output to support resolution. 

DTV focuses on structured, repeatable comparison cycles, helping teams validate results quickly and consistently across test iterations. 

Key Benefits of DTV 

  • Automated comparison reduces manual validation workload 
  • Pre-delivered SAP validation content accelerates setup 
  • Clear discrepancy identification supports rapid issue resolution 
  • Supports multiple test cycles before go-live 

DTV is especially valuable in system conversion projects where the expectation is that business results should match before and after technical transition. 

Validation as a Strategic Enabler 

Both LTVF and DTV reflect a broader evolution in SAP transformation programs: validation is no longer a last-minute activity. Instead, it is a strategic enabler that: 

  • Improves project transparency 
  • Reduces operational risk 
  • Shortens cutover windows 
  • Builds stakeholder confidence 

As SAP landscapes grow more complex, automated validation frameworks become essential components of transformation architecture rather than optional add-ons. 

 An Overview Comparison 

 

Criteria 

LTVF  

(Landscape Transformation Validation Framework) 

DTV  

(Data Transition Validation) 

Primary Purpose 

Validation for complex landscape and structural transformations 

Validation for system conversions and technical transitions 

Typical Use Case 

SDT, Consolidations, carve-outs, mergers, restructuring etc. 

System conversion or system upgrade 

SAP S/4HANA Conversion 

Can support all including complex selective transitions  

Supported for System Conversion from SAP ECC 6.0 to SAP S/4HANA 2020+ 

SAP S/4HANA Upgrade 

Supported 

Supported for upgrade from S/4HANA 1709+ to higher release 

Lean Selective Data Transition (SDT) 

Strong fit when complex changes apply 

Supported when using Business Transformation Cockpit (BTC) 

NZDT (Near Zero Downtime Technology) 

Supported 

Not supported 

Handling Aggregation / Merging 

Supported 

Limited 

Handling Renumbering / Mapping 

Extensive rule support 

Limited 

Tolerance Thresholds/Acceptance 

Supported 

Limited 

Cross-Table Validation 

Strong capability 

Basic table/ALV comparison 

Pre-delivered SAP Validation Content 

Yes (Reference Catalog is provided for different scenarios) 

Yes (Finance, MM, SD, etc.) 

Multi-System Validation 

Strong capability 

Primarily source → target 

Complexity Handling 

High-complexity transformations 

Medium complexity, standardized scenarios 

Implementation Effort 

Higher setup effort, tailored design 

Faster setup for limited standard content 

 Consultant Support 

From the time of signing until go-live 

From the time of signing until the first test cycle. 

Final Thoughts 

Landscape transformations and system conversions require more than technical delivery; they demand structured assurance that business data remains correct and reliable. 

  • LTVF provides a flexible, rule-driven framework suited for all scenarios including complex restructuring and multi-system transformation scenarios. 
  • DTV offers a standardized and automated validation mechanism tailored for system conversions, upgrades and lean selective data transitions, particularly in SAP S/4HANA projects. 

By integrating the right validation approach early in the project lifecycle, organizations can significantly reduce testing effort, minimize downtime, and ensure a smoother path to go-live. 

Some reference links: 

3271210 - BDV - Business Data Validation Service - SAP for Me 

LTVF: 

3271502 - LTVF - Landscape Transformation Validation Framework - SAP for Me 

Reducing Test-Effort and Downtime using the valida... - SAP Community 

DTV: 

3117879 - Data Transition Validation DTV Tool - Central Note - SAP for Me 

Business data validation using DTV tool - SAP Community 

An Introduction to the Data Transition Validation ... - SAP Community 

3258383 - DTV : SAP Pre- delivered Content - SAP for Me 

Mastering the Data Transition Validation Tool for SAP S/4HANA Tra 

Engagement with relevant SAP team or SAP Experts 

If your business requirement is to validate any transformation scenario you can engage with the SAP Data Management and Landscape Transformation (DMLT) consulting team through following ways; 

  1. Create a Support Incident 
    Submit an incident via the SAP ONE Support Launchpad under the component: CA-LT-SRV (Landscape Transformation Services), Or 
  2. Contact via Email 
    Reach out directly to the DMLT Global Consulting Experts Email: sap_dmlt_gce@sap.com 

Disclaimer: This blog is intended only for knowledge sharing about Business Data Validation according to the landscape transformation requirements. Any data validation efforts should be thoroughly assessed in consultation with qualified subject matter experts, supported by a comprehensive scope, and aligned with the appropriate approvals from your auditors and relevant stakeholders. Please note that the insights presented here reflect the tool capabilities available at the time this content was prepared.

entry.title: Company Code Merge using DMLT Business Transformation Service : A Comprehensive Guide
entry.content:
(The Power of One: Streamlining Success through DMLT )

Company Code Merge using DMLT Business Transformation Service : A Comprehensive Guide

Introduction It is noted that company code mergers are an essential component of enterprise transformation, often necessitated by legal compliance, restructuring, or mergers and acquisitions. Financial reporting is simplified through this consolidation, and DMLT Business Transformation services are utilized to streamline the planning, data migration, and support phases.

What is a Company Code Merge? A company code merge is defined as the process by which two or more legal entities are combined into a single unified company code within the system. Financial and master data are transferred into the new entity, while the legacy company codes are subsequently decommissioned,.

When to Use SAP Company Code Merge

  • Legal Mergers and Acquisitions: It is applied when legal entities merge or are acquired, ensuring the system reflects the new organizational structure.
  • Internal Restructuring: It is utilized to combine subsidiaries or divisions, achieving economies of scale and simplified management.
  • Tax or Regulatory Compliance: It is executed to consolidate unneeded company codes while retaining historical data for regulatory requirements.
  • Consolidation of Systems: It is performed when multiple legacy or departmental systems are centralized to reduce IT complexity.
  • Streamlining Financial Reporting: It is implemented to merge smaller legal entities, thereby simplifying the production of consolidated financial statements.

 

Benefits of SAP Company Code Merge

  • Streamlined Financial Reporting: The generation of reports is simplified, as all financial data is consolidated into a single entity.
  • Cost Savings and Efficiency: IT overhead and infrastructure costs are reduced by leveraging a unified operational system.
  • Improved Data Accuracy and Integrity: Data quality is enhanced, and the risk of duplicate records is minimized through centralized master data maintenance.
  • Enhanced Operational Efficiency: Business processes, such as procurement and sales, are standardized and processed more efficiently across the organization.
  • Reduced IT Complexity: System landscapes are simplified, leading to easier maintenance, fewer integration issues, and faster upgrades.
  • Compliance and Governance: Audit processes and regulatory filings are streamlined by utilizing a unified accounting framework.
  • Strategic Agility: Real-time, comprehensive data access is provided to management, enabling faster strategic decisions.

Process of Merging Company Codes

 

 

  • Step 1: Pre-Planning and Analysis
    • Scope Definition: The target company code, required data transfers, and project timelines are identified by relevant stakeholders.
    • Impact Analysis: The operational effects on financial statements, IT systems, and compliance are evaluated to formulate mitigation strategies.
    • Gap Analysis: Discrepancies in system configuration and master data between source and target codes are identified for resolution.
    • Project Plan and Roadmap: Detailed tasks, timelines, and team responsibilities are established across all project phases.
    • Change Management: Organizational impacts are addressed through targeted communication and user training preparations.
  • Step 2: Clean-Up and Prepare Data
    • Finalize Go-Live: A cut-off date is established, after which no new transactions are permitted in the source systems.
    • Clear Open Items: Outstanding invoices and unclarified items are settled to ensure accurate balance sheet transfers.
    • Reconcile Intercompany Transactions: Financial balances between the merging entities are settled to prevent double-counting.
    • Archive or Clean-Up Old Data: Historical data is archived or deleted according to retention requirements, often supported by DMLT tools.
    • Data Quality Checks: The completeness and accuracy of all master and transaction data are verified prior to the migration.
  • Step 3: Configure the Target Company Code
    • Align Configuration: Financial settings, such as charts of accounts and currencies, are aligned with the source codes.
    • Replicate Master Data: Master records are created or mapped in the target system to match the source data parameters.
    • Assign Organizational Units: Relevant organizational structures, such as plants or sales areas, are properly assigned to the target code.
    • Transfer Customization: Custom configurations and system settings are migrated and updated to ensure target compatibility.
    • Update Interfaces and Integrations: External system interfaces are redirected to communicate with the new legal entity.
    • Transformations Requirements: Additional technical transformations, such as the merging of controlling areas, are completed if necessary.
  • Step 4: Perform the Data Transformation
    • Migration/Transformation Approach finalization: The transformation approach is decided, potentially including on-the-fly transformations utilizing DMLT services.
    • Extract Data from Source Systems: All relevant financial and master data is extracted as of the established cut-off date.
    • Transform and Validate Data: Data is mapped to the target format and thoroughly validated against the source totals.
    • Load Data into Target System: The transformed data is imported into the target system using batch jobs or specialized DMLT tools.
    • Post Migration Adjustments: Outstanding entries are posted and missing master records are corrected via system adjustments.
  • Step 5: Testing and Validation
    • Reconciliation Testing: Migrated balances and operational transactions are comprehensively reconciled with the source data.
    • Functional Testing: Key business processes are tested to confirm that expected functionalities operate smoothly.
    • System Integration Testing: Data flow and interactions with all integrated external systems are verified.
    • User Acceptance Testing (UAT): Daily operational tasks are executed by end-users to confirm system readiness.
    • Performance Testing: System capacity is evaluated to ensure high transaction volumes can be handled post-merge.
    • Compliance and Audit Checks: The accuracy of financial records and the existence of auditable migration trails are validated.
  • Step 6: Final Cutover and Rollout
    • Freeze the Source Systems: Source systems are locked against new transactions following successful testing phases.
    • Perform the Cutover: Ongoing business operations are redirected entirely to the unified target system.
    • Unfreeze the Target System: Posting periods are opened in the target system to allow for normal operational transactions.
    • Communicate the Change: Internal stakeholders and users are formally informed of the successful system transition.
  • Step 7: Post-Implementation Support
    • Monitoring and Support: Immediate post-merge support is deployed to resolve any arising operational issues.
    • Data Archiving: Archiving of legacy data from the old company codes is conducted using appropriate tools.
    • Decommission the Source Company Codes: Old company codes are fully deactivated in accordance with recommended system procedures.
    • Review and Continuous Improvement: A post-implementation review is conducted to document lessons learned and refine future processes.

SAP S/4HANA and Data Model Changes

  • Universal Journal (ACDOCA): It is required that all historical transactions from legacy codes be loaded into the unified journal structure.
  • Single Business Partner: Customer and vendor records are mapped and consolidated into a unified Business Partner model.
  • Document Splitting: Target configurations are adjusted to ensure financial transactions are split according to new reporting requirements.
  • System Landscape: DMLT services and conversion tools are utilized to ensure a cinsitent and reliable transformation. 
  • Post-Merger Configuration: New consolidation setups and reporting views are established for the merged financial entity.

Key Considerations and Challenges

  • Legal and Regulatory Compliance: Adherence to standard accounting principles and data protection laws must be ensured.
  • Data Integrity and Accuracy: Thorough validation is required to prevent incorrect reporting resulting from migration errors.
  • Master Data Harmonization: Overlapping customer and vendor records must be resolved to create a consistent set of master data.
  • System and Process Impact: Affected business processes and external interfaces must be identified and integrated seamlessly.
  • Downtime and Business Continuity: Operational disruption must be minimized through coordinated cutover strategies and phased rollouts.
  • Organizational Change Management: User adoption is improved by executing comprehensive communication and training plans.
  • Technical Challenges: Complexities regarding custom code, high data volumes, and overall system performance must be addressed.
  • Post-Merger Adjustments: Strategic plans must be established to handle reporting differences and opening balance adjustments.
  • Audit and Compliance: Process transparency must be maintained to ensure all data transfers satisfy external audit requirements.
  • Cost and Resource Management: Project budgets, timelines, and technical resources must be carefully managed to prevent operational delays.

Conclusion

  • Strategic Alignment: The transformation is planned in consultation with key business units to align with organizational goals.
  • Thorough Planning: Scopes and impacts are detailed early in the process to effectively manage expectations.
  • Data Management: Data accuracy and integrity are maintained through the use of DMLT   Solutions.
  • Change Management: Smooth system adoption is achieved by involving stakeholders and addressing concerns early.
  • Use of Tools: High-quality migration outcomes are secured by leveraging specialized DMLT tools.
  • Continuous Improvement: Ongoing system optimization is fostered by conducting detailed post-implementation reviews.
